caveats: a: OEWS is for NAICS 313300 and therefore includes fabric coating mills.
a: Published occupation shares are employment-weighted; wage weighting is approximated from the displayed wage structure rather than observed at 313310.
a: The defect-detection review demonstrates technical capability across textiles, not realized labor substitution in US finishing mills.
rho: The small-business survey spans industries and measures generative-AI use or plans rather than production automation.
rho: Labor savings may be absorbed as throughput or quality capacity rather than headcount reduction.
rho: Customer qualification and environmental controls can slow otherwise feasible deployments.
e: No public source measures lens eligibility in the specified EBITDA band.
e: The provided LMM firm count is an estimate and may include integrated or converter operations.
e: Environmental liabilities can impair transferability even when customer revenue is otherwise eligible.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes, not NAICS 313310 or the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: Owner plans may not be completed and may involve partial or non-control changes.
s5: A family transfer qualifies only if control and the operating business actually transfer.
q: No public evidence measures five-year retained AI benefit for textile finishers.
q: Converter wholesale margins behave differently from contract finishing fees, which is why the lens is narrowed.
q: Environmental or energy savings may be shared differently from direct labor savings.
d5: The output series includes NAICS 313320 fabric coating mills.
d5: Industrial production is realized US output rather than direct demand for the frozen service basket.
d5: Future trade policy and customer reshoring decisions are not forecast explicitly.
o: This estimate is conditional on the demand represented in d5; it does not reapply volume decline.
o: Some laboratory, color-development, inspection, and documentation work can be self-served even though the physical process remains external.
o: Environmental compliance makes an accountable operator necessary but can also push customers toward alternative supply.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Mostly per-yard, per-pound, per-lot, per-garment, or program-based job finishing for fabric mills, apparel manufacturers, brands, and industrial-textile customers; converter revenue may include a wholesale spread on purchased grey fabric. Specifications, approved recipes, color matching, compliance, and turnaround create repeat relationships but limited contractual subscription revenue.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 313310 combines outsourced finishers with converters that buy grey goods, outsource finishing, and resell at wholesale. The lens is narrowed to repeat external-customer finishing programs so those distinct operating models are not treated as one service population.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive finishing inside integrated mills, converters whose economics are principally speculative wholesale inventory rather than repeat customer programs, fabric coating mills, apparel manufacturing, one-off project shops without repeat demand, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing dyeing, bleaching, printing, washing, preshrinking, mercerizing, napping, scouring, cleaning, and other textile or fabric finishing to external B2B customers, including converters with durable repeat finishing programs.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the broader NAICS 313300 group, which combines finishing and coating, rather than a wage-weighted task-exposure share for 313310. The estimate applies partial AI exposure to office support, sales, planning, recipe and process analysis, visual inspection, documentation, and selected supervisory work while treating most chemical handling, machine tending, maintenance, setup, and material movement as physical. Coating occupations in the broader group may modestly distort the mix in either direction.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 313300 and therefore includes fabric coating mills.
Published occupation shares are employment-weighted; wage weighting is approximated from the displayed wage structure rather than observed at 313310.
The defect-detection review demonstrates technical capability across textiles, not realized labor substitution in US finishing mills.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 10-15 independent US finishers, with current automation segmented across color matching, lab dips, recipe development, inspection, scheduling, compliance records, customer service, machine tending, and rework, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: The broader industry has 12.81% of employment in office and administrative support, 5.95% in management, 4.11% in sales, 3.39% in business and financial operations, and 5.14% in inspection. These functions contain substantial information processing, while computer vision can automate parts of defect inspection. Yet 56.59% of employment is production, including 17.10% in bleaching and dyeing machine operation, so exposure is bounded by physical process work and accountable chemical and quality control.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production measures real output for NAICS 3133, combining textile finishing and fabric coating, rather than constant-quality demand for the current repeat-service basket of NAICS 313310. The index fell from 66.3922 in 2022 to 60.3558 in 2025. The range projects a moderated decline rather than mechanically extending the full recent contraction, because surviving US finishers may benefit from quick-turn, specialty, regulated, and nearshore supply. Coating demand may differ from finishing demand, creating error in either direction.
caveats: The output series includes NAICS 313320 fabric coating mills.
Industrial production is realized US output rather than direct demand for the frozen service basket.
Future trade policy and customer reshoring decisions are not forecast explicitly.
d5.change_evidence: NAICS 313310 real shipment volume by process and end market, utilization and backlog data from independent finishers, comparable import penetration, and five-year sourcing plans from major fabric, apparel, and industrial-textile customers would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The recent real-output series supports a below-one base: output declined in 2023, 2024, and 2025 and remains far below 2021. Demand persists because downstream textile products still require dyeing, printing, washing, and functional finishes, but import penetration and contraction in domestic upstream textile and downstream apparel production remain serious pressures. Specialty work, shorter lead times, traceability, and customer compliance requirements support the upper case.

caveats: No public source measures lens eligibility in the specified EBITDA band.
The provided LMM firm count is an estimate and may include integrated or converter operations.
Environmental liabilities can impair transferability even when customer revenue is otherwise eligible.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 118 estimated LMM-band firms showing contract-finishing share, repeat revenue, top-customer concentration, converter inventory exposure, permits, PFAS history, wastewater treatment, equipment ownership, and stand-alone management would directly determine eligibility.
e.rationale: Contract finishing is explicitly part of the industry definition, and approved colors, recipes, compliance capabilities, and turnaround performance commonly support repeated B2B programs. Most stand-alone LMM finishers should therefore fit the external-customer lens. Eligibility is reduced for captive units, inventory-led converters, highly concentrated or customer-dependent shops, distressed facilities, and businesses where wastewater, PFAS, real-estate, or deferred-equipment liabilities make the operating business nontransferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 313310 ($944,709,258) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 313310 ($4,187,450,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $277,609 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 313310); class <5: 279 firms, avg revenue/firm $508,534 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $77,551 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 97 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,802,289 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $274,849 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,633,683 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $401,637 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 89 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,548,685 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,151,175 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,277,759 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,837,358 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $96,146,850 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,662,395 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 118

caveats: This estimate is conditional on the demand represented in d5; it does not reapply volume decline.
Some laboratory, color-development, inspection, and documentation work can be self-served even though the physical process remains external.
Environmental compliance makes an accountable operator necessary but can also push customers toward alternative supply.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing interviews and category-level data separating continued third-party finishing from vertical integration, imported prefinished materials, and on-demand digital processes would test how much volume still requires an independent domestic operator.
o.rationale: Dyeing, bleaching, printing, washing, and mechanical finishing transform physical materials through equipment, chemistry, heat, water, handling, and accountable quality control. Software can automate recipes, inspection, scheduling, and documentation but cannot itself execute the finishing process. The residual displacement risk is customer vertical integration, standardized digital processes that shift work abroad, or a move to prefinished materials, rather than pure software self-service.

caveats: No public evidence measures five-year retained AI benefit for textile finishers.
Converter wholesale margins behave differently from contract finishing fees, which is why the lens is narrowed.
Environmental or energy savings may be shared differently from direct labor savings.
q.change_evidence: Contract and quote data from representative mills showing price-reset cadence, open-book costing, surcharge clauses, rework responsibility, customer concentration, win-loss reasons, and post-automation margin evolution would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Savings from lower rework, fewer shade failures, reduced waste, faster approvals, better scheduling, and avoided overhead hiring are not always visible as billable labor and can remain with the finisher during existing price cycles. Retention erodes when per-yard or per-lot prices are rebid, customers use open-book or cost-plus terms, or competitors deploy similar tools. Scarce recipes, qualification histories, fast turnaround, compliance performance, and specialty effects improve bargaining power relative to commodity finishing.

rho.bridge: A national small-business survey reports current or planned generative-AI use, and textile research documents computer-vision defect detection, but neither measures the share of exposed labor opportunity implemented in NAICS 313310 over five years. The estimate translates broad adoption intent and technical feasibility into workflow implementation, then discounts for legacy machinery, sensor and data integration, color and substrate variation, validation requirements, capital limits, wastewater compliance, and customer approval. Broad low-cost GenAI uptake likely overstates shop-floor implementation.
caveats: The small-business survey spans industries and measures generative-AI use or plans rather than production automation.
Labor savings may be absorbed as throughput or quality capacity rather than headcount reduction.
Customer qualification and environmental controls can slow otherwise feasible deployments.
rho.change_evidence: Installation-level evidence from US finishers and their equipment, color-management, ERP, and machine-vision vendors showing adoption, false-reject rates, approved recipe changes, hours saved, rework, wastewater impacts, and payback periods would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Finishing mills can implement low-capital tools in quoting, specifications, customer communication, scheduling, purchasing, reporting, and compliance documentation faster than closed-loop process control. AI inspection is technically credible, but reliable color and defect performance across fabrics, dyes, lighting conditions, and short production runs requires data, cameras, integration, and human exception handling. Regulated effluent and possible PFAS scrutiny raise the cost of poorly controlled process changes.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years; among employer owners age 55 or older the share was 30%. These are plans across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers by LMM finishing mills. The estimate excludes unconsummated plans, non-control transactions, closures, and transfers lacking a viable stand-alone operation, while allowing genuine family, management, or third-party control transfers. Capital intensity and environmental diligence likely make completion harder than for the average employer firm.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes, not NAICS 313310 or the LMM EBITDA band.
Owner plans may not be completed and may involve partial or non-control changes.
A family transfer qualifies only if control and the operating business actually transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A decade of closed control sales, family and management transfers, closures, owner ages, and withdrawn sale processes for US independent textile finishers would permit a direct five-year transfer hazard.
s5.rationale: Employer firms are materially more transferable than nonemployers, and an aging owner base creates real five-year supply. For finishing mills, however, wastewater compliance, possible legacy PFAS exposure, old equipment, customer concentration, and working-capital needs can turn intended transfers into delayed sales or closures. The base is therefore slightly below the broad employer-owner plan rate.

principal_driver: Repeat outsourced finishing programs contain measurable quality, rework, scheduling, documentation, and administrative workflows that AI can augment.
principal_weakness: Contracting domestic output and potentially severe environmental liabilities can overwhelm otherwise credible operating efficiencies.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as textile, fabric, and apparel finishing plus converters buying grey goods, having them contract-finished, and wholesaling them; lists core finishing processes.
sources.title: 313310: Textile and Fabric Finishing Mills - Census Bureau Profile

sources.supports: Reports the broader group's occupation mix, including production at 56.59%, office support at 12.81%, bleaching and dyeing operators at 17.10%, and inspectors at 5.14%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Textile and Fabric Finishing and Fabric Coating Mills

sources.supports: 2025 review documenting computer vision, image processing, and machine learning for faster and more reliable textile defect detection, with continuing implementation challenges.
sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Driving Innovation in Textile Defect Detection

sources.supports: Reports that 57% of surveyed small-business owners currently use or plan to implement GenAI within a year, led by content, data and information, and marketing uses; gives survey population and methodology.
sources.title: U.S. Bank 2025 Small Business Survey

sources.supports: Describes finishing processes and regulated pollutants, and states EPA is studying PFAS use and discharge because some textile-mill wastewater samples contain PFAS and most mills are not monitoring for it.
sources.title: Textile Mills Effluent Guidelines

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years, rising to 30% among employer owners age 55 or older; provides survey methods.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Federal Reserve real-output index for the broader industry: 66.3922 in 2022, 61.8800 in 2024, and 60.3558 in 2025.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Textile and Fabric Finishing and Fabric Coating Mills (NAICS 3133)

title: Textile and Fabric Finishing Mills
